title: Bahrain Preview: The Championship Is Ferrari's to Lose — But There's a Queue Forming Behind Them
meta: Published: 09 April 2026 | By Camille Dubois, Opinion Columnist | F1CC News
caption: Ferrari lead the standings — but the chasing pack is tightening as the paddock heads to Bahrain.
body:
Four races in, and the 2026 championship already has a shape. Ferrari lead. Audi are the surprise package. Mercedes are fighting themselves as much as the clock. Red Bull and McLaren are nowhere. And Eetu Väisänen, with 64 points and a 23‑point cushion, is the man everyone is chasing to Bahrain. But here’s the thing: that cushion should feel comfortable. Japan proved that comfort is an illusion. Ferrari’s strategy gamble backfired spectacularly at Suzuka, handing Audi a maiden victory and allowing the pack to close. Väisänen finished seventh, and if Robin Keisner can win a grand prix from tenth on the grid, then no lead is safe. The Finn has been the benchmark, yes — two wins, two pole positions, flawless in the wet and the dry. But Bahrain is a different beast: abrasive asphalt, searing heat, a circuit that devours rear tyres. If Ferrari get the strategy wrong again, the conversation shifts.

Ferrari’s advantage is built on the versatility of their car — a machine that conquered Melbourne’s floods, Shanghai’s strategic chessboard, and Suzuka’s damp chill. Yet each of those victories required a specific kind of genius: the patience to wait on hard tyres while others burned through softs, the nerve to hold a wet‑weather line while rivals spun. Bahrain demands something else entirely. The Sakhir circuit is a rear‑limited track, where rear tyres overheat in the high‑speed sweeps and suffer under traction out of the slow corners. Last year, Ferrari’s chronic weakness — high track temperatures — resurfaced here, costing them a potential win. The Scuderia have worked hard on their cooling package and suspension geometry over the winter, but Bahrain’s 50‑degree track surface will provide the sternest test yet. If Väisänen can win here, the championship lead becomes structural. If he can’t, the chasing pack — led by the astonishing Robin Keisner — will scent blood.

The Audi Problem

Robin Keisner is second in the championship with 41 points. Let that sink in. A driver who finished 19th in 2024, 17th in 2025, and started this season as an afterthought is now the closest challenger to the most dominant car on the grid. His Japan win was not a fluke — it was the culmination of a four‑race run that included a sprint podium in China, a last‑to‑fifth charge in Shanghai, and a controlled, opportunistic victory at Suzuka. Audi are second in the constructors’ standings, only 14 points behind Ferrari. They have a car that warms its tyres faster than anyone else in cool conditions — a trait that turned the Japanese GP in their favour. Bahrain’s heat will test whether that advantage translates, but the trend is unmistakable. Keisner has momentum, and momentum is the most dangerous thing in this sport. Yet the question that nags at every paddock observer is whether Audi can sustain this. Their two‑car performance in Japan, with Richard Hicks adding an eighth‑place finish, showed genuine depth. If they can replicate that in the desert, Ferrari will be looking over their shoulders all season long.

The Midfield Bloodbath

Behind the top two, the fight is savage. Eren Aygen sits third with 34 points, level with Wesley Vandesteene on 33 and Quarter Kevu on 28. Aygen, the Williams driver who spent years in the wilderness after being dumped by Red Bull, has become the grid’s most consistent performer outside of Ferrari. Vandesteene, after a brilliant Melbourne podium, has ridden a rollercoaster of lock‑ups and recoveries. Kevu, the reigning champion, is grinding out results without ever looking truly comfortable in the VCARB. The Racing Bulls car is quick but twitchy; Kevu’s racecraft is keeping him in contention, but the spark of his title‑winning 2025 campaign has yet to ignite. Michiii MaraDöner — still only 18 — has 27 points and a rookie fearlessness that makes him a threat every weekend. He’s not just fast; he’s audacious, and Bahrain’s wide, flowing layout will encourage his overtaking instincts. Sarah Springfield has dragged the Aston Martin to 24 points, a number that flatters the car but not the driver. She has a habit of delivering in chaos, and Bahrain’s twilight race — with temperatures plummeting as the sun sets — could open another door. Zhao Lei, eighth with 23 points, is doing what he always does: quietly accumulating while the chaos rages around him. The Chinese driver is the championship’s silent accumulator, and he rarely makes mistakes.

That’s six drivers separated by 11 points, all fighting for the final podium steps. Bahrain’s layout — three long straights, heavy braking zones, plenty of overtaking — will shuffle this order further. Someone will emerge as the clear third force. Someone else will lose ground. The championship won’t be decided here, but the hierarchy behind Väisänen and Keisner will start to harden.

The Crisis Clubs

Red Bull are seventh in the constructors’. Jason Roy has 15 points, Philippe Verhaeren 10. This is a team that won races last season and arrived in 2026 expecting to fight for the title. Instead, they are behind Williams and Alpine. Roy’s Q1 exit in Japan was the nadir — his worst qualifying performance in recent memory — and his race ended in a crash that nearly collected Sam Fakt. The Red Bull looks fast in a straight line but unstable under braking, and the strategy calls have been questionable. Bahrain, with its frequent safety cars and tyre‑wear puzzles, is exactly the kind of race where Red Bull could sneak a result — or sink deeper. Roy is a proud competitor, and a driver of his calibre will not tolerate anonymity much longer. But the car needs to deliver a clean weekend first.

McLaren are ninth. Ninth! Zhao Lei has salvaged 23 points through consistency, but Joshua Nathanial is still scoreless after four rounds — a statistic so brutal it defies explanation. He was taken out in Japan through no fault of his own, but the zero in the points column is becoming a weight. The car has pace, as Nathanial’s P3 qualifying in Japan proved, but translating that into Sunday results is now an urgent priority. McLaren cannot afford to be behind Haas in the standings for much longer, especially with the upgrades Mercedes has brought to the power unit. Bahrain’s heavy traction demands will test whether the chassis can match the engine’s improved deployment. If Nathanial can simply finish in the points, it will feel like a victory.

And then there is Afonso Cachinho. Zero points. Six penalty points. Another Q1 exit in Japan. The Ferrari second seat is the most scrutinised cockpit on the grid, and the driver filling it has not delivered a single meaningful result. Randy Belacik’s patience, publicly expressed but visibly thinning, will not last forever. Bahrain is a pressure cooker, and Cachinho is already simmering. If he can’t score points here — on a track where the Ferrari should be strong — the questions about his future will grow from whispers to headlines. The paddock has a long memory, and a driver who can’t deliver in the best car is a driver who won’t keep it for long.

Ferrari’s Livery Gamble

Amid the competitive tension, a lighter note: paddock rumours suggest Ferrari will unveil a special livery for the Bahrain Grand Prix. Details are scarce — the team has not commented officially — but the whispers point to a tribute to the Gulf region, possibly incorporating gold accents or a retro nod to Maranello’s past. Special liveries have become a quiet tradition in modern Formula 1, and Ferrari’s marketing department knows the value of a headline that isn’t about penalty points. Whatever the design, it will be the most photographed car on the grid — and the one everyone expects to see on the top step come Sunday. In a season already rich with narrative, a visual statement from the Scuderia would only deepen the sense that they are the team to beat.

What Bahrain Will Tell Us

Bahrain is the first race of the season on a permanent circuit in hot conditions. Melbourne was wet, Shanghai dry but cool, Suzuka cold and damp. The Middle Eastern heat will expose any car that struggles with rear tyre degradation. Ferrari’s Achilles’ heel in recent years has been exactly that — overheating the rears in high‑temperature races. If they survive Bahrain with a win, the championship lead starts to look structural. If they struggle, the door opens for Audi, Mercedes, and the chasing pack.

The fight for the podium, the crisis at Red Bull and McLaren, Cachinho’s future, and the silent pressure on drivers like Nathanial and Zwart — all of it will play out under the desert lights. Bahrain is the bridge between the flyaway openers and the heart of the European season. By Sunday night, the championship picture will have shifted again. The only question is who will be smiling, and who will be reaching for the excuse book. Four races in, the 2026 season is already delivering on every promise. The next chapter begins under the floodlights. So here’s the question you might be afraid to answer: can Ferrari hold off the pack, or is the ground already shifting beneath them?
